cards.text: Adult diabetes fell to 15.6% in 2023, down from 18.3% in 2019, and headlines called it the epidemic easing.
cards.sub: One survey down does not make a trend. The number that sets tomorrow's diabetes moved the other way.
sources: NHMS 2023 Fact Sheet (IKU); NHMS 2019; corroborated by CodeBlue 16 May 2024

cards.h: Overweight and obesity climbed to 54.4% of adults in 2023, up from 50.1% (2019), 47.7% (2015) and 44.5% (2011).
cards.s: More than one in two adults, about 11 million people, now carry excess weight, the strongest driver of type 2 diabetes.
cards.d: Overweight and obesity climbed to 54.4% of adults in 2023, up from 50.1% (2019), 47.7% (2015) and 44.5% (2011). More than one in two adults, about 11 million people, now carry excess weight, the strongest driver of type 2 diabetes.
sources: NHMS 2011/2015/2019/2023; CodeBlue 16 May 2024; FMT 16 May 2024; Sinar Daily (11M adults)

cards.h: Two in five Malaysians with diabetes do not know they have it. Among those aged 18 to 29, about 84% are unaware.
cards.s: The headline rate counts the diagnosed. The real burden hides in the undiagnosed, and it skews young.
cards.d: Two in five Malaysians with diabetes do not know they have it. Among those aged 18 to 29, about 84% are unaware. The headline rate counts the diagnosed. The real burden hides in the undiagnosed, and it skews young.
sources: NHMS 2023 Fact Sheet (IKU); The Vibes; CodeBlue

cards.h: One in two adults lead sedentary lives, and about 84% take part in no sport, fitness or leisure activity.
cards.s: Physical inactivity is the modifiable input feeding the weight curve, and it sits upstream of every clinic visit.
cards.d: One in two adults lead sedentary lives, and about 84% take part in no sport, fitness or leisure activity. Physical inactivity is the modifiable input feeding the weight curve, and it sits upstream of every clinic visit.

cards.text: Diabetes is the lagging indicator. Body weight is the leading one, and it has risen at every survey since 2011.
sources: Editorial synthesis from NHMS trend series

cards.text: Judging the epidemic by one survey is like calling the tide by a single wave. The waterline that decides the next decade, the share of adults overweight, has risen at every reading.
A health system can lower a diabetes number for a year. It cannot outrun a population that grows heavier at every survey.

sources: NHMS 2011/2015/2019/2023 (MOH/IKU); Scientific Reports 2025 methodology paper; CodeBlue/Galen Centre; FMT; The Vibes; Sinar Daily. See engine/briefs/diabetes-obesity-nhms-signal.md.
confidence: Medium-High. The overweight+obese monotonic series (44.5 to 54.4%) and the undiagnosed-share figures are corroborated across multiple outlets citing NHMS. The unverifiable 'changed measurement definition' claim was DROPPED at the accuracy gate, so no card asserts WHY the 2019 to 2023 diabetes figure fell. Primary IKU/MOH PDFs returned 403 to automated fetch; open manually before publishing.
verification_notes: Stage 2 (Bias) and Stage 3 (Fact) reviewers: please specifically (a) confirm the overweight+obese series values for each NHMS year against the primary fact sheet, (b) confirm the 2-in-5 undiagnosed and 84% (18-29) figures, and (c) CHECK whether any primary source states the 2019 (18.3%) and 2023 (15.6%) diabetes figures used different case definitions / are non-comparable. We could not verify a definition change and therefore did not claim one; if you find a primary source either way, flag it.
